coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of sulfadiazine via g-C3N4/carbon dots nanosheets under nanoconfinement: Synthesis, Biocompatibility and Mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Yi
Zhou, Shukui
Deng, Lin
Shi, Zhou
Jiang, Haiyan
Zhou, Shuai
coredata.dc:description:
A metal-free environmentally-friendly and highly efficient photocatalyst is always desirable for photocatalytic remediation of antibiotic contamination. Herein, we firstly reported a novel g-C3N4/carbon dots nanosheets (C-CN-NS) nanocomposite photocatalyst which was prepared through a simple thermal oxidation etching process to remove a representative antibiotic sulfadiazine (SDZ). The degradation rate of SDZ by C-CN-NS500 (calcination temperature at 500 °C) was around 2.4 times larger than that of g-C3N4/carbon dots (C-CN) under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation. The valence band of C-CN-NS500 increased by 0.42 eV under the quantum confinement effect, which promoted the oxidation potential, in favor of the degradation of SDZ. Increased specific surface area, improved separation efficiency and prolonged contact time for C-CN-NS500 can also facilitate the degradation of SDZ. Experiments demonstrated that photo-induced hole (h+) has the strongest effect in photocatalytic degradation process, followed by
O2 −, and
OH. The toxicity assessment showed that C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite has high biocompatibility and low toxicity. This work may provide a promising green photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic contaminant.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Nanosheets
Photocatalysis
Sulfadiazine
Nanoconfinement
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the bulk C-CN and C-CN nanosheets
Evaluation of the photocatalytic degradation of SDZ
Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of SDZ

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of C-CN. (b) TEM image of C-CN-NS. (c) AFM image and (d) the corresponding section analysis of C-CN-NS.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) the corresponding pore-size distribution curves for C-CN, C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450, C-CN-NS500 (The inset of image shows the volume for the different samples).
XRD patterns (a), Raman spectra (b), FT-IR spectra (c), C 1s (d) and N 1s (e) XPS spectra of C-CN and C-CN-NS500 samples.

(a) Degradation of SDZ by different photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. (b) First-order kinetic rate constants of SDZ degradation for different photocatalysts. (c) Cycling tests of the C-CN-NS500 towards SDZ degradation. (d) (e) (f) FT-IR, high-resolution C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of C-CN-NS500 before and after 5 cycles of photocatalytic degradation.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) fluorescence emission spectra, (c) transient photocurrent responses, (d) and photoluminescent lifetime decay of C-CN, C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450 and C-CN-NS500, respectively.

Spin-trapping ESR spectra of C-CN-NS500: (a) in methanol dispersion for DMPO-
O2 − and (b) in aqueous dispersion for DMPO-O
H. (c) Photodegradation curves of SDZ with addition of scavengers over C-CN-NS500. (d) Room temperature ESR spectra of C-CN and C-CN-NS500.
A possible mechanism for the enhancement of photocatalytic degradation of SDZ by C-CN-NS500.
Relative viability of A549 cells after incubation of 24 h with different concentration of C-CN and C-CN-NS500.
Textural parameters and elemental compositions for C-CN and C-CN-NS500.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of sulfadiazine via g-C3N4/carbon dots nanosheets under nanoconfinement: Synthesis, Biocompatibility and Mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Water Engineering and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A metal-free environmentally-friendly and highly efficient photocatalyst is always desirable for photocatalytic remediation of antibiotic contamination. Herein, we firstly reported a novel g-C3N4/carbon dots nanosheets (C-CN-NS) nanocomposite photocatalyst which was prepared through a simple thermal oxidation etching process to remove a representative antibiotic sulfadiazine (SDZ). The degradation rate of SDZ by C-CN-NS500 (calcination temperature at 500 °C) was around 2.4 times larger than that of g-C3N4/carbon dots (C-CN) under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation. The valence band of C-CN-NS500 increased by 0.42 eV under the quantum confinement effect, which promoted the oxidation potential, in favor of the degradation of SDZ. Increased specific surface area, improved separation efficiency and prolonged contact time for C-CN-NS500 can also facilitate the degradation of SDZ. Experiments demonstrated that photo-induced hole (h+) has the strongest effect in photocatalytic degradation process, followed by
O2 −, and
OH. The toxicity assessment showed that C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite has high biocompatibility and low toxicity. This work may provide a promising green photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic contaminant.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfadiazine (SDZ) has been extensively used in both human and veterinary medicine to prevent and inhibit bacterial infections [1]. Yet, it cannot be thoroughly metabolized by the most living organisms due to the p-aminophenamide in the SDZ molecule [2]. In recent years, sulfadiazine has been detected in municipal wastewater treatment plants [3], groundwater, and even drinking water by hydrologic cycle [4]. The persistent existence of SDZ in the aquatic environment increases the frequency of antimicrobial resistance genes in bacteria, posing serious threats to human, animal and ecosystem [5]. Therefore, it’s urgently required to explore novel environmentally compatible and energy-efficient approaches for the removal of SDZ.

To date, various techniques including ferrate (VI) [6], Fenton [7], ozone [8] and persulfate [9,10] have been applied in the field of SDZ remove. However, the mentioned approaches suffer from secondary pollution associated with sludge, high energy consumption and poor economically viable. Photocatalysis has been regarded as one of the most prospective technology owing to economic feasibility and environmentally friendly conversion by solar energy [11].

Over the past few decades, organic semiconductors including covalent organic framework [12], conjugated microporous polymer [13], covalent triazine framework [14], and carbon nitrides [15–18] have been exploited as metal-free photocatalysts. Among these, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been considered to be an appealing metal-free visible-light-induced organic semiconductor photocatalyst thanks to its impressive characteristics of earth abundant elements, facile synthesis, and high physicochemical stability [19,20]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of pristine g-C3N4 is limited by high recombination rate of photo-induced carrier and narrow absorption spectrum of visible light [21]. Thus, in order to address above-mentioned limitations, great efforts have been made, such as nanostructure design, elemental doping, copolymerization and the development of g-C3N4 based hybrid heterojunctions [22–27].

As an ecofriendly carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) cannot only suppress the recombination of photo-induced hole-electron pairs, but also can enlarge the optical absorption range [28,29]. A metal-free carbon dots/g-C3N4 composite was fabricated and proven to be an impressive photocatalyst for water splitting [30,31]. Our previous research confirmed that the incorporation of CDs to g-C3N4 resulted in an improved photocatalytic degradation rate for SDZ under visible light [32]. The as-synthesized in-plane interconnected carbon dots/g-C3N4 heterostructures (C-CN) displayed 3.2-fold higher kinetic rate for SDZ degradation than pure g-C3N4. What’s more, it’s reported that carbon nitride nanosheets exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than the bulk g-C3N4 due to shorter bulk diffusion length, larger specific surface area and the effect of quantum confinement [33–35]. Therefore, it is worth exploring the photocatalytic performance of the exfoliated C-CN for SDZ removal.

Herein, we report a thermal oxidation etching method for fabrication of C-CN nanosheets (C-CN-NS) which are applied to the photocatalytic degradation of SDZ under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic efficiency of C-CN and its nanosheets toward SDZ were evaluated under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). In order to reveal the enhanced photocatalytic activity mechanism for SDZ, the morphology, crystalline form, chemical structure and composition, optical and photo-electrochemical properties of the prepare C-CN-NS were characterized in detail. In addition, the stability and biocompatibility of C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite were also investigated. We expect that this work could offer a method for the degradation of antibiotic contaminant.

SDZ was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea, ethylene glycol, ascorbic acid, benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were used as provided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the bulk C-CN and C-CN nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Bulk C-CN powder (3C-CN) was synthesized according to our previous research [32]. The C-CN nanosheets were prepared via the following procedure: 500 mg of the bulk C-CN were covered in a combustion boat, then heated at 400 °C, 450 °C, 500 °C for 2 h in air with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, respectively. The faint yellow powdery C-CN nanosheets (C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450, C-CN-NS500) were finally obtained with a yield of about 76.0 %, 40.5 %, 19.1 %, respectively.

The performance of the as-synthesized materials was evaluated through the photodegradation of SDZ under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic experiments were performed using a photoreactor, which was illuminated by a 500 W Xenon lamp (CEL-S500, CEAULIGHT, Beijing) with a filter (λ < 420 nm). For each test, 24 mg of photocatalyst was put into 60 mL of 10 μM SDZ aqueous solution and agitated for 30 min in darkness to achieve an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 0.5 mL suspension was periodically taken out and immediately filtered by 0.22 μm syringe filters for SDZ concentration determination. The pH of SDZ aqueous solution was 5.7 ± 0.2, and each test was conducted in triplicate. The methods for the determination of SDZ concentration, characterizations and cytotoxicity assay are provided in Text S1, Text S2 and Text S3, respectively.

The morphologies of C-CN and C-CN-NS500 were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1 a and b). As displayed in Fig. 1a, a black curly laminar morphology denoted as multi-layers structure for C-CN is observed. In contrast, the exfoliated C-CN (C-CN-NS500) exhibits a grey flat laminar morphology denoted as fewer-layers structure as shown in Fig. 1b. The porous structure and coarse surface of C-CN-NS500 also increase the specific surface area. For better understanding of the thickness and morphology of C-CN-NS500, atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurement was implemented, and the results are depicted in Fig. 1c and d. The thickness of C-CN-NS500 is determined to be around 1.0 nm, suggesting that the as-prepared C-CN-NS500 is four-layer structure (the interlayer distance is 0.324 nm obtained from the XRD pattern). The above analysis confirms that C-CN nanosheets were successfully synthesized.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of C-CN, C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450, and C-CN-NS500 are shown in Fig. 2 a. According to the IUPAC classification, all samples display type IV isotherms with a H3-type hysteresis loop, revealing that all samples are mesoporous materials [36]. The BET surface area (SBET) of C-CN, C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450, and C-CN-NS500 are calculated to be 75.5, 111.8, 173.7, and 258.8 m2/g, respectively. It is obvious that the thermal oxidation etching could significantly improve the SBET of C-CN, and increases with the rising of exfoliation temperature. Moreover, as represented in Fig. 2b, the volume of samples increases with the enhancement of the exfoliation temperature under the identical mass. The C-CN-NS500 exhibits a relative broad pore size distribution (1–40 nm) with micropores (1.2 nm) and mesopores (2.52 nm) and quite big surface area for each pore size. The micropores and mesopores could be caused by the splitting of the multi-layers C-CN during the thermal exfoliation process. Therefore, the SBET of C-CN nanosheets increases along with thermal exfoliation at high temperature, which is attributed to the reduction in thickness of bulk C-CN.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns shows that C-CN and C-CN-NS500 possess graphitic stacking structures with (100) diffraction peaks at 12.9° and (002) peaks at 27.5° as displayed in Fig. 3 a. The (100) and (002) peaks corresponds to the in-plane structural repeat units of tri-s-triazine and interlayer stacking of aromatic structures, respectively [19]. The distance between the stacking of the conjugated aromatic system is calculated to be 0.324 nm using Bragg’s equation (d=λ/2sinθ). In addition, the (002) peak intensity of C-CN-NS500 is stronger than that of C-CN, implying more regular repetitions between graphitic layers [37]. In other words, C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite has better crystallization which to the benefit of the photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectra of C-CN and C-CN-NS500. Characteristic peaks at 708 and 1233 cm−1 are attributed to the breathing mode of heptazine ring and the stretching vibration modes of CN and CN heterocycles of g-C3N4 [38], respectively. And the peak intensity of C-CN-NS500 is higher than that of C-CN, suggesting that C-CN-NS500 condenses more heptazine ring, which is consistent with XRD analysis. In addition, compared to C-CN, the peaks intensity at 1357 (D band), 1573 (G band) cm−1 of carbon dots is enhanced for C-CN-NS500, and the peak at 1491 cm−1 related to semicircle stretching of carbon atoms in fused aromatic rings is also significantly enhanced, suggesting that the C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite has more graphitic carbon and carbon of aromatic rings [39].

Furthermore, the surface functional groups of C-CN and C-CN-NS500 were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR). As displayed in Fig. 3c, the characteristic bands of the C-CN-NS500 are identical to that of the C-CN, suggests that the primary functional groups of them are the same. The chemical states and compositions of C-CN and C-CN-NS500 were further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. 3d and e. Compare to C-CN, the peak intensity at 284.8 eV of C 1s spectrum for C-CN-NS500 related to graphitic carbons [30] is enhanced, suggests that the surface of the as-synthesized C-CN-NS500 possesses more graphitic carbons. In addition, the atomic percentages of C and N also measured by XPS, and the surface atomic ratio of C to N are calculated to be 0.732 and 0.864 for C-CN and C-CN-NS500, respectively. At the same time, the atomic ratio of C to N determined by elemental analysis are 0.678 and 0.679 (Table 1 ) for C-CN and C-CN-NS500, respectively. This result further corroborates that the surface of C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite has higher carbon content in the form of graphitic carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the photocatalytic degradation of SDZ
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the potential performance of C-CN and exfoliated C-CN, the degradation of SDZ is proceeded under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, SDZ was hardly decomposed without photocatalyst after 60 min visible light irradiation as blank test, suggesting that SDZ is a stable organic compound without self-photolysis. In dark condition, 9 % of SDZ was removed within 30 min, implying that the adsorption capacity of photocatalysts is very limited. As expected, the combination of photocatalysts and visible light irradiation resulted in an increased SDZ degradation. When the exfoliation temperature exceeded 400 °C, the C-CN nanosheets displayed higher photocatalytic activity for SDZ than that of C-CN. Among a series of photocatalysts, C-CN-NS500 presented the highest photocatalytic efficiency, in which 97.82 % SDZ could be removed after 60 min visible light illumination. Furthermore, the degradation process of SDZ is described by the pseudo-first order kinetics model. The kinetics constant k obtained by C-CN, C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450, and C-CN-NS500 are 0.026, 0.019, 0.035, and 0.063 min−1, respectively (Fig. 4b). The kinetics constant initially decreases, then increases with rising of the exfoliation temperature. The C-CN-NS400 presents the lowest rate constant, which could be attributed to the structure of it to facilitate recombination of photo-induced carriers.

In general, stability and reusability are significant parameters for practical application of photocatalyst. Accordingly, consecutive experiments were performed to investigate the stability and reusability of C-CN-NS500. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of SDZ for C-CN-NS500 decreased after first run, then remaining stable, the degradation efficiency of SDZ was still achieve to 87.53 % at fifth run, and higher than that of C-CN. This phenomenon could be attributed to the decrease of specific surface area, resulted from the π-π stacked aggregates of C-CN-NS500 nanosheets after the first reaction. Besides, no obvious changes are observed in FT-IR, C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of C-CN-NS500 after five times reuse (Fig. 4d–f), demonstrating a very stable physicochemical structure.

The UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra in Fig. 5 a clearly shows that the intrinsic absorption edge of the prepared samples exhibit a blue shift at gradually elevating exfoliation temperature. According to Kubelka-Munk method (n = 1/2 for the indirect bandgap), the band gap energy (E g) is calculated to be 2.74, 2.90, 2.92 and 2.98 for C-CN, C-CN-NS400, C-CN-NS450 and C-CN-NS500, respectively. The blue shift of the fluorescence emission for C-CN nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 5b, further demonstrates that the E g of C-CN nanosheets is increasing with the rising of exfoliation temperature. The larger E g for C-CN nanosheets is ascribed to quantum confinement effect caused by the changes of the density of electronic states [40]. In addition, compare to g-C3N4 nanosheets with the thickness of around 2 nm and the bandgap increment of 0.2 eV [33], C-CN-NS500 with the thickness of around 1 nm has stronger quantum confinement effect resulting in the larger bandgap increment (0.24 eV).

Fig. S1a illustrates the Mott-Schottky plot of C-CN-NS500, the conduction band potential (ECB) of C-CN-NS500 is estimated to be -0.88 eV [41–43]. As shown in Fig. S1b, the valence band potential (EVB) of C-CN-NS500 is determined to be 2.10 eV [44]. Therefore, the Eg of C-CN-NS500 is calculated to be 2.98 by Eg= EVB - ECB, which is consistent with the Eg value (2.98 eV) derived from Kubelka-Munk method, indicates that the analysis of band structure for C-CN-NS500 is reasonable. At the same time, the EVB and ECB of C-CN are 1.68 and -1.06 eV [32], respectively. Compared to C-CN, the EVB value of C-CN-NS500 increases by 0.42 eV, and a higher oxidation potential which is benefit to degradation of SDZ, which is also attributed to the quantum confinement effect.

Transient photocurrent responses of the prepared samples under intermittent visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. 5c. Compared to C-CN, C-CN-NS500 nanocomposite exhibits obviously enhanced photocurrent responses. This result suggests that C-CN-NS500 can promote the separation efficiency of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. S2, the Nyquist plot of the C-CN-NS500 possesses a more depressed semicircle at high frequency, which reveals a lower resistance and a more efficient interfacial electron transfer over C-CN-NS500 among the prepared samples [45]. The improvement of photo-electrochemical properties for C-CN-NS500, could be attribute to more graphitic carbon in the surface and more active sites due to the larger specific surface area.

To evaluate the fluorescent lifetime and probe the electron-transfer process, the time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of the prepared samples were performed (Fig. 5d). The luminescence decay curves are fitted by a biexponential model, and the relevant parameters are shown in Table 2 . τ1 is derived from the nonradiative recombination of excitons due to the defect states of the samples, and the longer lifetime component of τ2 is attribute to the recombination of free charge-carriers in the samples [46]. Compared to C-CN, both components τ1 and τ2 of the C-CN nanosheets are prolonged except for C-CN-NS400, resulting in an increase in the average charge-carrier lifetime (<τC-CN> = 3.85 ns, <τC-CN-NS450> = 4.29 ns, <τC-CN-NS500> = 5.81 ns) (Table 2). This result, following the same trend as their photocatalytic performance, suggests that longer average charge-carrier lifetime for C-CN nanosheets facilitates the contact of photo-induced carriers and SDZ molecules, thereby improving the degradation of SDZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of SDZ

ce:section.ce:para:
To directly prove the degradation mechanism by reactive species (RS) in the photocatalytic process for C-CN-NS500, a series of electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy measurements and RS-scavenging experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 6 a–b, no ESR signals are detected in the dark. Yet, four typical characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − and DMPO-
OH with an intensity ration of 1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1 are observed with the light on for C-CN-NS500, respectively. According to band theory, C-CN-NS500 (ECB=−0.88 eV, EVB = 2.10 eV vs SHE) can reduce O2 to
O2 − (0.13 eV vs SHE) [47], but cannot oxidize water to
OH (2.68 eV vs SHE) [47]. Therefore,
OH was produced due to homolytic scission of H2O2 which derived from reduction of
O2 – [48].

Photocatalytic degradation performance with scavengers isopropanol (IPA, 10 mM), benzoquinone (BQ, 10 mM) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 10 mM) to quench photo-generated
OH,
O2 − and h+ [47], respectively are shown in Fig. 6c. The results imply that h+ has the strongest effect in photocatalytic degradation process, followed by
O2 –, and
OH plays a minimal role in this process.

In addition, the ESR spectra of C-CN and C-CN-NS500 are shown in Fig. 6d. Both C-CN and C-CN-NS500 display the paramagnetic signal at g = 2.003, corresponding to unpaired electrons within the π-conjugated aromatic rings [49,50]. Compared to C-CN, the ESR signal of C-CN-NS500 is strengthened, suggesting more optimum π-conjugated system [49,50], in favor of separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, which is in line with results of photo-electrochemical tests.

On the basis of the above results and analyses, a possible mechanism for the enhanced of photocatalytic degradation of SDZ over C-CN-NS500 is proposed as shown in Fig. 7 . Fewer-layers of C-CN-NS500 nano-composite was prepared by thermal exfoliation of multi-layers of C-CN. The specific surface area of C-CN-NS500 increased due to the porous fewer-layers structure and coarse surface, is almost 3.4 times than that of C-CN, which can provide more active sites, which facilitates the degradation of SDZ. At the same time, the band gap of C-CN-NS500 increased by 0.24 eV under the effect of quantum confinement, the correspondingly EVB value also increased by 0.42 eV. In other words, the oxidation potential was enhanced, which in favor of the degradation of SDZ.

In addition, the C-CN-NS500 condensed more heptazine ring, and the surface of C-CN-NS500 had more carbon of graphitic and aromatic rings. All these properties are favorable to form a big delocalized π-conjugated system, then accelerates electron transfer and lowers the photo-resistance, sequentially enhances the separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers, ultimately improves the photocatalytic activity for SDZ degradation. On the other hand, these properties can also prolong the luminescence lifetime of C-CN-NS500 due to the formation of a big delocalized π-conjugated system, then the contact time of photo-induced carriers and SDZ (or oxygen) molecules is accordingly extended. As a consequence, the degradation efficiency of SDZ is also promoted.

According to the photocatalytic products detected by High Performance Liquid Chromatography/Triple Quad Mass Spectrometry, the pathways of SDZ degradation are considered to be consistent with the previous report [32], 4-(2-iminopyrimidin-1(2H)-yl)aniline is the major product, and the products are favor of the aquatic environment safety.

To sum up, enhanced photocatalytic degradation of sulfadiazine by C-CN-NS500 due to the larger specific surface area, the higher separation efficiency, the longer residence time of photo-induced hole-electron pairs, and the quantum confinement effect.

In order to apply photocatalyst to natural water pollution, the toxicity for A549 cells of C-CN and C-CN-NS500 were investigated by MTT assay. A549 cells were cultivated in 5, 25, 50, 75 and 100 μg/mL of dispersed photocatalysts culture mediums, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 , the cell viabilities are inversely proportional to concentration of photocatalysts in general. The gap of toxicity between C-CN-NS500 and C-CN tends to decline with the increasing of photocatalysts concentration, and C-CN-NS500 was less lethal to A549 cells than C-CN at the scope of tests. Besides, cell viability significantly declines with the increasement of C-CN concentration, but the viability of A549 cells hindered by C-CN-NS500 keeps the relatively high value. The cytotoxicity grade of <3 according to the USP cytotoxicity grading scales [51], suggesting the high biocompatibility and low toxicity of C-CN-NS500 for A549 cells at the scope of tests.

In summary, C-CN-NS500 displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity and degradation efficiency of SDZ as compared with C-CN under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation, and the degradation rate of SDZ was around 2.4 times larger than that of C-CN. The EVB value of C-CN-NS500 increased by 0.42 eV under the effect of quantum confinement, promoted the oxidation potential, which is in favor of the degradation of SDZ. And increased specific surface area, improved separation efficiency and prolonged contact time for C-CN-NS500 can also promote the degradation of SDZ. The primary active species in the photocatalytic degradation process of SDZ by C-CN-NS500 are: h+ >
O2 − >
OH. The toxicity assessment shows that C-CN-NS500 has high biocompatibility and low toxicity. This work demonstrates that C-CN-NS500 is a promising green photocatalyst for SDZ degradation.

Yi Duan: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shukui Zhou: Conceptualization, Resources, Formal analysis. Lin Deng: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Zhou Shi: Supervision. Haiyan Jiang: Visualization, Investigation. Shuai Zhou: Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (No. 2020JJ5495), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (No. 18C0433), the Science and Technology Program of Hengyang City, China(No. 2019yj010770).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2020.104612.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tetrahydrobiopterin biosynthesis as an off-target of sulfa drugs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Haruki, M.G. Pedersen, K.I. Gorska, F. Pojer, K. Johnsson, Tetrahydrobiopterin Biosynthesis as an Off-Target of Sulfa Drugs, Science. 340 (2013) 987.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced degradation of sulfadiazine by novel β-alaninediacetic acid-modified Fe3O4 nanocomposite coupled with peroxymonosulfate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, X. Cui, X. Lu, X. Jian, Q. Xu, C. Tan, Enhanced degradation of sulfadiazine by novel β-alaninediacetic acid-modified Fe3O4 nanocomposite coupled with peroxymonosulfate, Sci. Total Environ. 662 (2019) 490-500.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fate of sulfonamides, macrolides, and trimethoprim in different wastewater treatment technologies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Göbel, C.S. McArdell, A. Joss, H. Siegrist, W. Giger, Fate of sulfonamides, macrolides, and trimethoprim in different wastewater treatment technologies, Sci. Total Environ. 372 (2007) 361-371.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation kinetics and mechanism of sulfadiazine and sulfamethoxazole in an agricultural soil system with manure application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: . Zhang, S. Hu, H. Zhang, G. Shen, Z. Yuan, W. Zhang, Degradation kinetics and mechanism of sulfadiazine and sulfamethoxazole in an agricultural soil system with manure application, Sci. Total Environ. 607-608 (2017) 1348-1356.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of wastewater treatment processes on antimicrobial resistance genes and their co-occurrence with virulence genes in Escherichia coli

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.K. Biswal, A. Mazza, L. Masson, R. Gehr, D. Frigon, Impact of wastewater treatment processes on antimicrobial resistance genes and their co-occurrence with virulence genes in Escherichia coli, Water Res. 50 (2014) 245-253.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yang, G.-G. Ying, J.-L. Zhao, S. Liu, L.-J. Zhou, F. Chen, Removal of selected endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) during ferrate(VI) treatment of secondary wastewater effluents, Water Res. 46 (2012) 2194-2204.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-F. Yang, S.-B. Zhou, A.-G. Xiao, W.-J. Li, G.-G. Ying, Chemical oxidation of sulfadiazine by the Fenton process: Kinetics, pathways, toxicity evaluation, J. Environ. Sci. Heal. B. 49 (2014) 909-916.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M.S. Hansen, A. Spiliotopoulou, R.K. Chhetri, M. Escolà Casas, K. Bester, H.R. Andersen, Ozonation for source treatment of pharmaceuticals in hospital wastewater – Ozone lifetime and required ozone dose, Chem. Eng. J. 290 (2016) 507-514.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: . Ji, Y. Shi, L. Wang, J. Lu, C. Ferronato, J.-M. Chovelon, Sulfate radical-based oxidation of antibiotics sulfamethazine, sulfapyridine, sulfadiazine, sulfadimethoxine, and sulfachloropyridazine: Formation of SO2 extrusion products and effects of natural organic matter, Sci. Total Environ. 593-594 (2017) 704-712.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activation of peroxymonosulfate by a novel EGCE@Fe3O4 nanocomposite: Free radical reactions and implication for the degradation of sulfadiazine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Tan, X. Jian, Y. Dong, X. Lu, X. Liu, H. Xiang, X. Cui, J. Deng, H. Gao, Activation of peroxymonosulfate by a novel EGCE@Fe3O4 nanocomposite: Free radical reactions and implication for the degradation of sulfadiazine, Chem. Eng. J. 359 (2019) 594-603.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Habibi-Yangjeh, S. Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, S. Feizpoor, A. Rouhi, Review on heterogeneous photocatalytic disinfection of waterborne, airborne, and foodborne viruses: Can we win against pathogenic viruses?, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 580 (2020) 503-514.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.S. Vyas, F. Haase, L. Stegbauer, G. Savasci, F. Podjaski, C. Ochsenfeld, B.V. Lotsch, A tunable azine covalent organic framework platform for visible light-induced hydrogen generation, Nat. commun. 6 (2015) 1-9.
